speaker: interviewer
text: It's November the 20th, 1996. I'm conducting an interview with Miriam Freedman. This is tape number five. You were back at school. Before you continue on that, can you tell me what sort of relationship that you had with your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was the youngest member of the family. I had a very special relationship with him. In a sense, because I was youngest, and I-- [LAUGH] there were so many women in our house, and my brother was away, who I adored, as well. I was very close to my brother. I was very close to my father. I was close to all members of the family. But these were two very powerful men in my life. And my late father tried to spoil me as much as he could. He used to just go away for work and, coming back, he tried to bring me something, bananas, sweet chocolate. You know, it wasn't so easy like today to get those things. Because they had to come from different countries-- fruit, oranges, and things like that. He tried to spoil me, in his own way. He used to take me in his lap, and he used to take me quite a lot. I had a lot of cuddles with him and, of course, walks. And, thinking back, he must have had so many problems, so many worries-- how to resolve our family problem. But he was always telling me stories and dream stories. Sort of wonderful things-- that life is going to be better, Messiah is going to come, and we are going to go to Palestine, and we're going to go to Israel-- what wasn't exist, in those days. And our life is going to be better. And we're going to have families gathering, and-- happy-- happy life, afterwards. Or a contented one. And he introduced me to the world of dream. Because I was kind of a dreaming person-- very dreamy. And whatever was wrong, he was always just telling me stories and stories and stories. And these are my memories of him. As a man who had dignity and faith and believed in future. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And life will be probably different.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said the-- or you were saying at the end of the last tape-- that your sister was trying to get you to come to Palestine.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Well, she tried to get us to Palestine during the occupation of Hitler in Slovakia. But after the war, she arranged for us very quickly to get our exit visa. There was a Czechoslovakian brigade organized to help to Palestine-- Israel, in those days. And, of course, we started to see what happened to us and how we could do something. We let ourselves to be slaughtered. You know, it was something quite new for everybody.

speaker: interviewer
text: So a small army was created. It was called the Czechoslovakian Brigade. And they went to help Israel, to liberate them for the British, Palestine. So we managed to get on a-- after some time-- a consultation, arrangement, I don't know how it was done. But we managed to get on one of the transport to go. We had permit. We were not illegal travellers. Do you know how long after the war that was?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it's '46. So it could be a year-- a year-- about a year after the war, yes. A little bit more than a year.

speaker: interviewer
text: One thing I forgot to ask you was do you remember the date that you were liberated?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know what the date was, but I presume it was sometime in May. I-- I can't really tell you exactly the date. But this is my recollection. It was in the spring.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm sorry to have interrupted the flow. So, back to your accounts of leaving for Israel.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my mother decided I'll dispose of the goods we had, certain things. Edith, my sister, was already living in Bratislava. She joined the Shomer Hatzair movement. And I was possibly belonging to some kind of youth movement, as well. That was just something to get us physically involved with. My sisters, Edith and Gertie and me-- we-- when we got our permit, we went via Paris. We went to Paris for a few days. And from Paris, we went to Marseille. In Marseille, we boarded a ship what took us to Palestine.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember that journey?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember the journey, and the name of the ship was I think so. And the captain and I, I remember, as well. He used to take a lot of people on the trip. It was a Greek ship. And he also spoiled me. Gave me an orange. I think that was the first orange a captain gave me. So the little kids are on the boat. I don't know what they looked like. And it was quite an interesting trip. But we found that there were a lot of illegal people on the boat as well.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it a crowded boat?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was fairly crowded. And we couldn't get used to the food. It was too greasy, I remember very strongly. But I think that [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] possibly that they tried to get some people off the ship and tried to get their permit-- and to pass it off to somebody else. I know that something was going on, but not too clearly. And, of course, my sister was waiting for us the other side.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did it take you to get there, do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: From Bratislava all the way-- I think it took between 10 days and two weeks. Because I remember, we stayed in Paris for a few days. And that was quite an horrendous experience, because the local people saw us on our lorries, and they're really waving at us like we were the liberators, or they knew that we were part of the group who suffered a lot. There was a lot of cheering going on in those days.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who had provided the lorries?

speaker: interviewee
text: I presume that, uh-- UNRRA-- some Jewish organization. I can't tell you. Presume they ran everything, how to.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it a rough journey on the sea?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a rough journey all along, yes. Rough on the lorries, and then, of course, on the boat, yes. It was quite a rough journey. But it was special.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the first day you arrived? Do you remember arriving in Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was terrible. There was a siren. There was a siren. Something was going on between the Israeli underground, Palestinian-- the Jewish underground, and the British. Yes, it was again, we were told some problem. A war is going on. That was very strange. Yeah. And then we got off the boat, and we went to-- in Haifa, we went to some center for-- we could have probably stayed and would have found accommodation, but my sister took us to Jerusalem, to her place. She was married, by that time, to Dr. Joseph Hermann. And they lived there, and we stayed there for a while. And then she moved--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember that reunion?

speaker: interviewee
text: With my sister? Yes, yes, it was wonderful. She was the eldest, and I was the youngest. Yes, it was. Of course, I did not know her husband at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you remember her?

speaker: interviewee
text: Vaguely. Because we spoke a lot about her, and of course, she was kind of a heroine for us. She managed to get away. Yes, I-- I had a vague picture. I really loved her, and I respected her a lot, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did this help your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, well, of course. One of her daughters was all right. Of course it helped. It was difficult to-- of course she was very happy to see her married and alive and well. But she lost three important people in her life.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you with your sister for?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we stayed in her place, and she moved out. And then eventually she moved to Tiberias, and we stayed there for a while. And then later, we moved to near Haifa, to Kiryat. Bialik and Kiryat Shmuel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go to school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure, I went to boarding school right away.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was the boarding school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my mother was still-- had no choice, because she-- we were not very much-- we were not settled there. So first I went to a place called Kfar HaNoar HaDati-- no, uh, Kfar Hasidim. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Uh-huh. It was Kfar Hasidim. And in Kfar Hasidim was a little boarding school called Kfar HaNoar-- Kfar HaNoar HaDati. And I stayed there for five years.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it far from where your mother was living?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I was separated again from my mother, because a war started. I was in-- I was separated from my mother for a year. I did not know if she's alive or not, and she didn't know about me. Well, I presume she thought she knew where I was, but there was no communication.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was it that it was arranged for you to go this boarding school? Who arranged for that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think the Jewish agency must have arranged it. She-- may have discussed it with her. There was first-- I was in another place-- [NON-ENGLISH]. It was a village with a family. And they  had a family, and they were quite nice to me, but I wasn't very happy. And there, I started to go to school. And then they decided to send me to Kfar HaNoar, where there was a boarding school and people from my background. People who went through the war. And my sister stayed with my mother in Jerusalem, and she went to a [INAUDIBLE] school. And I--

speaker: interviewer
text: Which-- which sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: Gertie. Gertie. Yes, she stayed in-- in Jerusalem. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And Edith went to the kibbutz. She survived. And when she got to Israel, Malia, she went to kibbutz Nir David. And she stayed there. We were, all during the war, separated. She also was in [NON-ENGLISH], the youth movement. And she stayed there, and she got married to somebody who survived the Holocaust, from Poland-- Israel Glaser. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I was very, very fond of him, Israel. He was a great, very special person, really. Again, another figure. I had very few male. But he was a very unusual man. And he liked me a lot, and I was very, very fond of him. And he tried to represent something-- the male part in my family.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you in Israel for?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in a boarding school for five years. And then I went to Jerusalem. I had a scholarship in a teaching training college. And then I volunteered to the Israeli army. I didn't have to go into the army, because I was Orthodox. But I was not in a uniform. I was on an unusual mission. And this is quite strange. I volunteered probably unconsciously. I volunteered to work with immigrant who arrived from Morocco and Iraq. And-- and I worked in a very primitive settlement. I worked with children. By that time, I was qualified already as a kindergarten teacher and a junior teacher. I was working for two years in this settlement with these children. I could probably identify with their background, because I also suffered a lot. And that was part of my army service. It was a choice, to be in a regular army or what they called [SPEAKING HEBREW]-- border settlement. And that was on the Syrian-Lebanese border.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that took up nine years of your life? Five years in boarding school--

speaker: interviewee
text: Five years in boarding school, three in the training college, and two years in the army.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old were you then?

speaker: interviewee
text: After the army? I must have been 22.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to you then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then I went back to-- we already lived in Kiryat Bialik, at that time. And I got a job in-- I worked in a city called Nahariya, as a head teacher in a kindergarten. And then, also, in the next-door school, I was teaching. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] in that time period, when I stayed in Jerusalem, I met some people from Belfast, strangely enough, who worked in the Jewish agency. And they introduced me to them. And they asked me, am I willing to go as the first Israeli emmissar representative to Northern Ireland. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was a very responsible job, like an ambassador, but it wasn't that capacity. I was only 23 years old. And I wanted to get out. I don't know-- I can't explain. I wanted to explore the world. And this came my way. And 1958-- December the 14th-- I sailed, and I got to Belfast. And it was quite a strange experience, again. I came to work in a country that I knew nothing about.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you speak English?

speaker: interviewee
text: I could speak English, not very well. But, you know, I learned in the school, in my boarding school. And we always learned English in school, but I had very bad English teachers, but I could speak. But not to the level that I expect-- they were expecting of me to teach in kindergarten, in a junior school, in university. I was taking over all the education in Northern Island-- the Jewish education-- teaching Hebrew. It was quite difficult. There was a rabbi, Alexander Carlebach, there, at that time, who I lived in his house for a while, and then I moved to a boarding school with Mrs. Wiener. She was a very sweet woman. And I did my work. And I went to English classes. And I worked there for nearly two years-- just under two years.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there anyone special that you met during that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I met my husband there. [LAUGHS] While I was there, I was also traveling in Europe a few times, on my own, for adventure, as in today. And I met my husband at a party in Dublin. Because I was working as a Jewish agency representative in Northern Ireland. And there was another couple who were representing Ireland, southern Ireland. And I made a connection because we were only us working there for the Jewish agency. I was the first one, actually, who ever entered to that country. And we created a connection.

speaker: interviewee
text: They introduced me at a party. I met my husband, really, in a party, but they sort of carry on the meeting place. And so I got married in 1959. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Cannot be. Doesn't sound right, does it? December. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] What year did you go to Ireland?

speaker: interviewee
text: '58, '59? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I have to work it out, don't I? Yes, I think the end of '59, or '60.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long have you been married, now?

speaker: interviewee
text: 36 Years.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it must have been '60. It's '96, now.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, well, my son was born in 1960. I think I was in Belfast-- anyway, a year and a half, I was alone in Belfast.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you get married in Belfast?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I got married in Haifa and lived there for a while. A few months. And then--

speaker: interviewer
text: How come you went back to Haifa?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that was one of my conditions, when I decided to get married. I was a great Zionist. And my husband found it a bit difficult to settle down, so we came to England.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you come back to England again?

speaker: interviewee
text: We came to England in the '60s. My son was born. And a year after my marriage, my son was born.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Martin Solomon. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And five years afterwards, my daughter was born. [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was she born?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was born in London, and he was born in Ireland. I was there during my confinement. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My husband's Irish, obviously, Irish.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you came back from Haifa, where did you live?

speaker: interviewee
text: We lived for a while-- since then I went to Dublin, stayed in my in-laws, and then, for a few months, till Martin, my son, was born, and then we came to London. Had a couple of flats before we bought a house.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did you come to London?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because my husband got a job in London. He wanted to live there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did he do, at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, originally he was an actor. He was trained as an actor. Then he was in his father's business.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Which was fancy goods, wholesale fancy goods. He was working with his brothers in the company. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he was interested in medicine, for some time, but he was too much involved with the Zionist movement during Israeli independence, so he left everything and went to live in Israel for a few years. And afterwards, he was trained as a chiropodist for three years, a foot doctor. And he worked since then, and now he's retired.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are your children married?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: What sort of work do they do?

speaker: interviewee
text: My son was trained as an electronic engineer. Then he took another degree, second degree, in artificial intelligence. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And at the moment, he's working in [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] financial [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] addictions. And he's very interested in mathematics. He really is a mathematician. So he also got understanding in--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where does he live?

speaker: interviewee
text: He lives in Houston, Texas. He is here at the moment. And my daughter was trained as a special-need teacher. She was also trained in drama for many years, acting, singing, dancing. She'd done some plays. And then she was trained as a special-needs teacher. And she's interested in all other different things. She started in London the Jewish Spiritual Network, and she's involved in all kind of creative things.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before we sort of bring this to a close, is there anything that you've forgotten that you'd like to add?

speaker: interviewee
text: No-- I-- only thing I would like to add, that I was a very sick person during the war, after the war. But in 1967, '67, I met a very special person, Morris Goldman, who was a great inspiration to my life. He was my first yoga teacher. But he was not just a yoga teacher, trained. He was a writer, he was a psychoanalyst, he was a chemist. He was an incredible person with a great influence in my life, who introduced me to alternative ideas and healings. Through him and through his influence, he-- I started to take interest [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in alternative ideas, basically, to heal myself. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I met some incredible and very, very special people on this journey. He was the first one. There was another lady, called Irina Tweedie. Margaret Simpson. Jenny Miller, who left a great mark on my spiritual journey.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have a message or something you would like to leave for future generations, about the Holocaust?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it's a prayer, probably. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It's a prayer-- a message to humanity. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] A war of this kind and tragedy of this kind, your mind can't even believe it-- not imagine it, that one human being can do to another what happened on this-- during this last war-- not only Jews, but also gypsies and Russians and other people suffered during this tremendous, tremendous six-day war. Six-year war. And my prayer is that people should read about it, learn about it, go to exhibitions, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and understand the value of human life. How precious, how beautiful a life is. And people should start to understand that we have to change our consciousness [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and understand that only through studying and understanding and interaction and get rid of prejudices and religious differences, we can overcome [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] hatred. We should always plant seed, where is hatred, with love. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And only through love, compassion, understanding, sharing, giving, we can really possibly change and create a better world. And there is no point, really, to anything else, unless we will create a harmonious world [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and help the underprivileged.

speaker: interviewer
text: We must not forget what's going on in the world. In every period, a holocaust is going on at the moment, as well-- in Zaire, in Rwanda. Last year was a holocaust in Yugoslavia. And people should see that somehow these things are still repeating itself. How can the world sit and watch? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So my prayer is always, wake up in the morning and thank God that you can see, walk, and talk and give your life to humanity and help people on the journey. Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you like to introduce your family for us, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Behind me is standing my husband, Leonard Freedman. And on my left is my daughter, Alison Naomi Freedman.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Would you like to tell me, what attracted you to your wife?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I found her full of zest, with a love of life, and terribly exuberant. And I was completely overwhelmed. I had no choice but to marry her.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you think your life would have been any different if your mother hadn't had the experience that she'd had during the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: For sure, but it's almost like saying, if a person's a different person, how would they bring you up? Everything that we go through influences who we are and then how we treat other people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, I appreciate that. But do you see any things that were different in your life, compared to maybe friends of yours whose parents hadn't been through the Holocaust?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, but in subtle-- it's subtle things.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you don't feel that it's affected you drastically.

speaker: interviewee
text: I mean, yes. I mean, it probably has affected me drastically. But I can't compare-- I have nothing to compare it to.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. Is there anything you'd like to add at this point?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm sure that, of course, you know, you can't help it, being a Holocaust survivor, how much you try to adjust to everyday life, you still have all your baggage on you, and you can't help it. You know, it comes out in a different way, not the way exactly you think it should come out. But something from the past, a certain amount of disturbance comes, from time to time. Obviously, you do things you shouldn't do, but it's something you-- maybe it happens to people who didn't go through the Holocaust, as well. I can't tell you. But I personally think maybe I've done certain things that possibly if I'd had a normal upbringing, I wouldn't do. It's difficult for me to tell you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything you would like to add?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Well, of course, it has made a difference, actually. And I always try and remember the background. And it's not always easy in everyday matters between a husband and wife, but I think that we've learned to accept that there are times when she reacts to different things in a certain, specific way because of her experiences. And that's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, not at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you like to tell us about this picture, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my grandfather's uncle, Chaim Tzvi Mannheimer. He was a prominent rabbi, the turn of the century. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He unified all the different friction within the Jewish community.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know when the picture was taken? What day?

speaker: interviewee
text: I do not know when the picture was taken. I presume-- this-- we found it, really-- it was given to me through one of my cousins in Switzerland. I do not know when the picture was taken and how old it is.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us about these people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, these are my grandparents, my father's parents. Pearl Mannheimer and Moshe Mannheimer.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know where this picture was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Again, I don't know where it was taken. But it was taken somewhere in Slovakia, turn of the century.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us about these pictures?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. This is my grandparents on my mother's side, Gitl Paskus and Joseph Paskus. And the photograph must have been taken, according to the costume, in the-- in the '20s or 30s.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us about these pictures, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: These are my parents, Olga Paskus-Mannheimer, my mother, and Solomon Mannheimer, my father.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know when they were taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't know when they were taken, but I think this picture was in the 1920s, of my mother. And the picture is a bit later, of my father.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us about these pictures, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother and my sister. On the right is my sister, Naomi. She perished on an Eichmann march. And Moshe Mordechai Mannheimer, my late brother, who died in Auschwitz a few days before the liberation. This photograph was taken before they went off to Hungary. It must have been 1942.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us about this picture, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, this is three of my sis-- three of us. And my eldest sister, my older sister, Edith, on the right Naomi in the middle, and myself, Eva, on the bottom. I think it was taken about in 1940.
